title: Ayenia sambiranensis
taxonomy: Ayenia sambiranensis is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malpighiaceae, commonly known as the Barbados cherry family. This family consists of approximately 130 genera and over 1,500 species, many of which are found in tropical regions. The Malpighiaceae family is often characterized by its unique combination of characteristics, including the presence of a distinct style and the lack of a distinct calyx. Ayenia sambiranensis is a member of the genus Ayenia, which consists of approximately 70 species found primarily in tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas. Further classification of Ayenia sambiranensis is typically done using more specific characteristics, such as its leaf shape and flower morphology.

intro: Ayenia sambiranensis, a species of flowering plant in the family Malpighiaceae, is a shrub native to Madagascar. Traditionally, this plant has been used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including inflammation, fever, and skin infections, due to its reported anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Ayenia sambiranensis include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Ayenia sambiranensis is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, as well as being used in the production of extracts and essential oils, to support its traditional uses and promote overall well-being.
